sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 10 of about 30 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the maze structure more clearly. Looking at the ascii:
Left corridor (cols 9-28):
- The left blue square is at rows 39-43, cols 19-23 (marked as S)
- Above it, the corridor goes up through cols 14-18 (rows 29-38), then left to cols 9-13 (rows 24-28), then up through cols 14-18 (rows 19-23), then left again (rows 14-18), then up (rows 9-13)
- Below it, the corridor goes down through rows 44-58 (all black - blocked!)
Wait, looking more carefully:
- Left square: rows 39-43, cols 19-23
- Below the square (rows 44-58): all black (BBBB...) - the bottom is blocked
- Above the square: rows 29-38 have BBBBBBBBBB at cols 9-18, then the square at 19-23, then YYYYY at 24-28
Hmm, the blue squares are in chambers. The left square is in a chamber bounded by:
- Left: black wall (cols 9-18)
- Right: yellow background (cols 24-28) 
- Top: black wall (rows 29-38, cols 14-18)
- Bottom: black wall (rows 44-58)
Wait, but the square moved up from rows 49-53 to rows 39-43. So there must be a path. Let me re-examine.
Looking at the left side more carefully:
- Rows 39-43: BBBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYYY (black wall cols 9-18, blue square cols 19-23, yellow cols 24-28)
- Rows 29-38: YYYYYBBBBBYYYYYYYYYY (yellow cols 9-13, black cols 14-18, yellow cols 19-28)
So above the blue square at rows 39-43, the area at rows 29-38 has yellow at cols 19-28. This means the blue square CAN move up into the yellow area! But when I tried UP, the board didn't change. 
Wait, let me re-read the output. The last UP had board_changed: False. But before that, the batch of 3 UPs moved the left square from rows 44-48 to rows 39-43, and the right square stayed at rows 44-48.
Actually wait, looking at the initial positions again:
- Initially: both at rows 49-53
- After first UP: both at rows 44-48
- After 3 more UPs: left at 39-43, right at 44-48
So the left square moved 5 rows up (from 44-48 to 39-43) and the right square stayed. This means the right square hit a wall.
Now the left square is at rows 39-43. Looking at the ascii, above it (rows 29-38) at cols 19-23 is yellow (background). So the square could potentially move up further into the yellow area. But the last UP didn't move it.
Hmm, maybe the blue squares can only move within the black structure (the maze corridors), not into the background areas. The black structure forms a maze, and the blue squares are constrained to move within the black corridors.
Let me look at the structure differently. The black shape (id=3) is a large connected component. The blue squares are "children" of this black shape, meaning they're enclosed by it. The black shape forms two U-shaped chambers connected at the top.
Let me trace the path more carefully. Looking at the ascii for the left side:
- Rows 9-13: black at cols 19-28 (top of left structure)
- Rows 14-18: black at cols 14-28 (extends left)
- Rows 19-23: black at cols 9-18 (extends further left)
- Rows 24-28: black at cols 9-13 (leftmost)
- Rows 29-38: black at cols 14-18 (middle-left wall)
- Rows 39-43: black at cols 9-18 (with blue square at 19-23)
- Rows 44-58: all black (bottom)
Wait, this doesn't make sense as a corridor. Let me look at where the YELLOW is (the open space inside the black structure):
Left side yellow regions:
- Rows 9-13: cols 9-18 (yellow)
- Rows 14-18: cols 9-13 (yellow)
- Rows 19-23: cols 19-28 (yellow)
- Rows 24-28: cols 14-28 (yellow)
- Rows 29-38: cols 9-13 and 19-28 (yellow)
- Rows 39-43: cols 24-28 (yellow)
So the yellow area forms a winding path. The blue square at rows 39-43, cols 19-23 is in a position where:
- To the left: black wall (cols 9-18)
- To the right: yellow (cols 24-28)
- Above: yellow (rows 29-38, cols 19-23)
- Below: black wall (rows 44-58)
So the blue square can move RIGHT into the yellow area, and UP into the yellow area. Let me try RIGHT.
Actually, I realize I need to understand the movement rules better. When I pressed UP, the blue squares moved up by 5 rows (the height of the square). This suggests they move in "steps" equal to their size. Let me try RIGHT to see if the left square can move right.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to see if the left blue square can move right
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
